5:06:45 memory, the document is represented by
5:06:47 the document object model. In the same
5:06:49 time, it also loads CSS as well. Most of
5:06:53 the resources that the HTML document
5:06:55 links to including embedded images,
5:06:58 videos, and even connected CSS are
5:07:00 subsequentially fetched by the browser

5:07:05 the process. We are not discussing
5:07:07 JavaScript here to make things easier
5:07:09 for you guys.

5:07:13 fetched and groups the various rules

5:07:18 use such as element, class, ID and so
5:07:21 on. It determines which rules should be
5:07:24 applied to which document object model
5:07:26 nodes based on the selectors. It
5:07:28 discovers and applies the appropriate
5:07:30 styles to each node as needed. This
5:07:34 immediate step is called render tree. So

5:07:41 the rules have been applied to it, the
5:07:43 render tree is organized in the manner

5:18:00 will learn what are CSS selectors and

5:18:03 let's start by discussing the agenda for
5:18:05 this tutorial. So in this tutorial we
5:18:07 will learn what are CSS selectors and
5:18:09 what are their different types. We will
5:18:11 also look at an examples to how to work
5:18:12 with CSS selectors. So what are CSS
5:18:16 selectors?
5:18:18 CSS selectors allow you to select and
5:18:20 style HTML elements. Selectors are used
5:18:22 to find elements based on their ID,
5:18:24 classes, types, attributes, value of
5:18:26 attributes and much more. Let's discuss
5:18:29 the most common selectors. There are
5:18:31 several different types of CSS
5:18:32 selectors. The most common of them are
5:18:34 CSS element selectors, CSS ID selector,
5:18:37 CSS class selector, CSS universal
5:18:39 selector and CSS group selectors. So
5:18:42 what is a CSS element selector?
5:18:45 The element selector selects element
5:18:47 based on the element name. This is a CSS
5:18:50 selector. It means it will select all
5:18:52 the P elements on the page. It will
5:18:54 style all the P elements to the central
5:18:56 array having blue color. This is a CSS
5:18:59 selector. It is set to P. This means it
5:19:03 will select all the P elements on the
5:19:04 page. It will style all the P elements
5:19:06 to be center aligned having blue color.
5:19:09 Let's look at what it looks on a page.
5:19:13 So as you can see all the elements are
5:19:15 centrally aligned with a text color
5:19:17 blue.
5:19:19 CSS ID selector. The ID selector uses
5:19:22 the ID attribute of an HTML element to
5:19:24 find a specific element. An ID should be
5:19:27 unique within the page. So you should
5:19:29 use the ID selector to find a single
5:19:31 unique element. To find an element
5:19:33 within a specific ID, use a hash
5:19:35 character followed by the ID of the
5:19:37 element. So here we have used the hash
5:19:41 paran. So all the rule will be applied
5:19:43 to the HTML element with ID equal to par
5:19:45 1. So this is an ID selector. It will be
5:19:48 applied to all the HTML element with ID
5:19:50 is equal to par 1. As you can see, it is
5:19:53 applied to the element having the ID
5:19:55 paravan and all the other elements are
5:19:57 defaultly styled. CSS class selector.
5:20:01 The class selector finds the element
5:20:02 with a specific class set by the HTML
5:20:04 class attribute. To select an HTML with
5:20:07 a specific class, use a period character
5:20:09 followed by the name of the class. So
5:20:11 this style will be applied to all the
5:20:13 elements having the class name class
5:20:15 one. This is a class selector. It is
5:20:17 denoted by a period symbol. All the
5:20:19 elements having a class name class one
5:20:21 will be styled with centrally aligned
5:20:24 and color blue. As you can see the
5:20:27 element having the class name class one
5:20:28 is styled and all the other elements are
5:20:30 set to default. CSS universal selector.
5:20:34 The universal selector selects all the
5:20:35 elements on a page. The universal
5:20:37 selector is assigned by a star
5:20:38 character. The universal selector
5:20:40 selects all the elements on a
5:20:42 stylesheet. As you can see it is denoted
5:20:44 by a star mark. All the elements inside
5:20:46 our web page will be centrally aligned
5:20:48 and the color will be set to blue. As
5:20:50 you can see, all the elements are
5:20:52 centrally aligned and the colors are set
5:20:54 to blue. CSS group selector. In
5:20:57 stylesheets, there are often elements
5:20:58 using the same style. To minimize the
5:21:00 code, you can group selectors. To group
5:21:02 the selectors, separate each selectors
5:21:04 with a comma. Here we have grouped
5:21:06 selectors for H1, H2, and P. So, style
5:21:10 is applied to H1, H2, and P elements
5:21:12 only. It is better to group the
5:21:14 selectors to minimize the code. Here we
5:21:16 have grouped selectors P, H2 and H3. So
5:21:19 style will be applied to only elements
5:21:21 having P, H2 and H3. As you can see, so
5:21:25 all the element of P, H2 and H3 are
5:21:28 centrally aligned and colored to red.
5:21:31 Every element that can be displayed on a
5:21:33 web page is comprised of one or more
5:21:34 rectangular boxes. CSS box model
5:21:37 typically describes how these
5:21:38 rectangular boxes are laid out on a web
5:21:40 page.
5:21:41 These boxes can have different
5:21:42 properties and can interact with each
5:21:44 other in different ways. But every box
5:21:46 has a content area and options
5:21:48 surrounding padding, border and margin
5:21:50 areas.
5:21:52 The following diagram demonstrates how
5:21:54 the content padding, border and margin
5:21:56 CSS properties determine how much space
5:21:58 an element can take on a web page. The
5:22:01 content area consists of content like
5:22:03 text, images or other media. It is
5:22:06 bounded by the content edge and its
5:22:07 dimensions are given by the content box
5:22:09 width and height.
5:22:11 The border area is the area between the
5:22:13 box padding and margin. Its dimensions
5:22:16 are given by the width and height of the
5:22:17 border.
5:22:20 The margin area consists of spaces
5:22:22 between the border and the margin. The
5:22:24 dimensions of the margin area are the
5:22:26 margin box width and margin box height.
5:22:29 It is useful to separate the element
5:22:30 from its neighbors.
5:22:33 The padding area is the area that
5:22:36 includes the element's padding. This
5:22:38 area is actually the space around the
5:22:40 content area and within the border box.
5:22:42 Its dimensions are given by the width of
5:22:44 the padding box and the height of the
5:22:46 padding box.
5:22:48 Now let's jump to an example to
5:22:50 understand the box model better. So we
5:22:53 are on the sublime text editor. To get
5:22:55 started I have created a box theme

5:26:13 What is a flexbox?
5:26:15 Flexbox also called flexible box model
5:26:18 is basically a layout mode or model that
5:26:20 provides an easy and clean way to
5:26:22 arrange the items within a container.
5:26:25 If you are using a CSS for a while, you
5:26:27 must have used the old block model where
5:26:30 you assign width in terms of percentage
5:26:32 or fixed width and then use the float to
5:26:35 arrange the items on the page. Suppose
5:26:37 for example you want to arrange three
5:26:40 boxes in a row. Then you have to enter
5:26:43 everything manually from width, height
5:26:45 and to padding. Flexbox can do all these
5:26:47 things with ease.
5:26:50 Some of the concepts of flexible box
5:26:52 model is the ability to alter the item
5:26:54 width and height to best fit in the
5:26:56 containers available in the free space.
5:27:00 Flex model is direction agnostic which
5:27:02 is different from the box model which is
5:27:04 vertically biased and the inline which
5:27:06 is horizontally biased. Flexbox works
5:27:09 for both.
5:27:11 Flexbox is more effective in a small
5:27:13 scale layouts.
5:27:16 So here are the main properties of the
5:27:17 flex.

5:33:03 What is a grid?
5:33:05 A grid is a set of intersecting
5:33:06 horizontal and vertical lines defining
5:33:08 the rows and columns.
5:33:11 And what is a grid layout? So CSS grid
5:33:15 layout is the most powerful layout
5:33:16 system available in CSS. It is a
5:33:19 two-dimensional system meaning it can
5:33:21 handle both columns and rows unlike
5:33:24 flexbox which is largely a
5:33:25 one-dimensional system.
5:33:27 You work with grid layout by applying
5:33:29 the CSS rules to both the parent element
5:33:32 which became the grid container and the
5:33:34 elements children which become the grid
5:33:36 items.
5:33:38 The CSS grid layout excels at dividing a
5:33:40 page into major regions or defining this
5:33:42 relationship in terms of size, position
5:33:44 and layer between parts of a
5:33:46 controlbuilt HTML primitives.
5:33:49 You can see this is the basic layout of
5:33:51 a grid system. Like tables, grid layout
5:33:54 enables an author to align elements into
5:33:57 columns and rows. However, many more
5:34:00 regular layouts are either possible or
5:34:02 easier with CSS grid than they were with
5:34:04 tables. For example, a grid containers
5:34:07 child element could position themselves.
5:34:09 So they actually overlap and layer
5:34:11 similar to the CSS positioned elements.
5:34:14 So here are the features of the CSS grid
5:34:16 layout. They are fixed and have flexible
5:34:19 track sizes. You can create a grid with
5:34:22 fixed track sizes using pixels. For
5:34:25 example, this sets the grid to the
5:34:27 specified pixel which fits to the layout
5:34:29 you desire. You can also create a grid
5:34:32 using flexible sizes with percentages
5:34:34 with the new FR unit designed for this
5:34:36 purpose.
5:34:39 You can place the items into a precise
5:34:41 location using the grid line numbers,
5:34:44 names or by targeting an area of the
5:34:46 grid.
5:34:47 The grid also contains an algorithm to
5:34:50 control the placement of items not given
5:34:52 an explicit position on the grid.
5:34:57 You can define an explicit grid with a
5:34:59 grid layout. The grid layout
5:35:00 specification is a flexible enough to
5:35:02 add additional rows and columns when
5:35:05 needed. Features such as adding as many
5:35:07 columns that will fit into a container
5:35:09 are included.
5:35:12 It also gives you control of overlapping
5:35:14 content. More than one item can be
5:35:16 placed into a grid cell or area and they
5:35:19 can partially overlap each other. This
5:35:22 layering may then be controlled with the
5:35:24 Z index property.
5:35:26 So here are the basic terminologies you
5:35:29 should know to work with the grid system
5:35:31 better.
5:35:34 The grid line is a dividing line that
5:35:36 make up the structure of the grid. They
5:35:38 can either be vertical or horizontal and
5:35:41 resides on either side of rows and
5:35:43 columns.
5:35:44 A grid column is a space between two
5:35:46 adjacent vertical grid lines. The size
5:35:49 of a grid column is determined by a grid
5:35:51 template columns property. On the other
5:35:53 hand, a grid row is a space between two
5:35:56 adjacent horizontal grid lines. Its size
5:35:59 is determined with a grid template rows
5:36:01 property.
5:36:03 The grid cell is the single unit of a
5:36:05 CSS grid.
5:36:08 And in grid design, a gutter is
5:36:10 basically the width of the space between
5:36:12 columns. A gutter margin is this white
5:36:14 space formed by the adjoining inside
5:36:16 margins of two facing pages in a book or
5:36:19 a space between columns on a page. So
5:36:23 with this, let's now move on to the
5:36:25 hands-on demo and see how the grid
5:36:27 system works in actual world.

6:08:58 Let's start this video with the
6:09:00 introduction to DOM. DOM stands for
6:09:03 document object model. It is an API for
6:09:06 HTML and XML documents which helps
6:09:10 JavaScript to understand what is there
6:09:12 written inside the HTML document. API
6:09:15 stands for application programming
6:09:17 interface to understand API better.
6:09:20 Let's take an example of a restaurant.
6:09:22 Imagine yourself in the restaurant. You
6:09:24 are sitting at the table with the menu.
6:09:27 The chef in the kitchen is also part of
6:09:29 the system. The waiter here works as a
6:09:32 messenger between you and the chef.
6:09:35 That's what API does. It works as a
6:09:38 moderator between JavaScript and the
6:09:41 HTML document. DOM defines the whole
6:09:44 HTML document in a hierarchical form to
6:09:47 make it understandable for JavaScript so
6:09:50 that other JavaScript functions can work
6:09:53 over it. It represents the entire
6:09:55 document in the form of object and nodes
6:09:59 whether it be elements or tags or
6:10:01 anything stored in the document. The
6:10:04 entire page content can be accessed or
6:10:06 manipulated by JavaScript. DOM is not
6:10:09 used with JavaScript only. Other
6:10:11 languages like Python also uses DOM. DOM
6:10:14 was designed to be used independently.
6:10:17 To begin with DOM, there is nothing
6:10:19 special you have to do. Different
6:10:21 browsers have different implementation
6:10:23 of the document object model. Every
6:10:26 browser uses DOM to make the web page of
6:10:29 the document accessible by JavaScript.
6:10:31 Let's move on and see what is document
6:10:34 object model with the help of an
6:10:36 example. I believe you guys have the
6:10:38 basic knowledge of HTML and CSS a
6:10:41 prerequisite for this course. Let's
6:10:44 start things off with a basic HTML
6:10:46 example. Here we have a code that
6:10:49 consists tags like HTML, head, body,
6:10:53 heading tag etc. We have the HTML tag
6:10:56 here, the opening HTML tag and the
6:10:58 closing HTML tag. Inside the HTML tag,
6:11:01 we have the head tag which contains the
6:11:04 document's header part. Inside the head
6:11:07 tag, there is a title tag that contains
6:11:09 the title of the web page. Then comes
6:11:11 the body tag which contains a heading
6:11:14 tag and two div tags with ids as div one
6:11:18 and div two. A div tag defines a
6:11:20 particular section in the HTML document.
6:11:23 The id used within the div tag. It is an
6:11:26 attribute basically that has to be
6:11:28 unique within the document to make the
6:11:30 element unique from other elements. Next
6:11:32 up we have a paragraph tag with a class
6:11:35 attribute within it. CSS and JavaScript
6:11:37 uses classes to select and access
6:11:40 specific elements. Don't worry guys if
6:11:42 you have no idea about it. Let us first
6:11:45 see the output of this code before going
6:11:47 to the document object model of this
6:11:49 whole document. So guys, this is the
6:11:51 output of the previous code we just came
6:11:53 through. Here we can see the title of
6:11:55 the web page as well as heading after
6:11:57 it. There were two different div tags
6:12:00 used in the code. Here's the output of
6:12:02 both the div tags. The question here is
6:12:05 where are those ID and class attributes
6:12:07 seen in the output. The answer to this
6:12:10 question is there's nothing on this web
6:12:12 page that can show both of them. We can
6:12:14 use them in our CSS file or through
6:12:16 JavaScript to specify the containers in
6:12:19 which they are used. So we have
6:12:21 concluded how a normal document works
6:12:24 and how the browser interprets the HTML
6:12:26 code. But how JavaScript uses this code
6:12:29 is a bit different. That's where DOM
6:12:31 comes into play. When the actual HTML
6:12:34 document is loaded in the browser,
6:12:36 another representation of that document
6:12:38 is created known as document object
6:12:40 model. Here's the model you can see on
6:12:42 your screen. Each of the tags used in
6:12:45 HTML document is correspondingly
6:12:48 represented as objects in the DOM like
6:12:51 starting from the HTML tag and covering
6:12:53 all the other tags like head, title,
6:12:56 body and few more. The same hierarchy as
6:13:00 there was in the HTML document goes with
6:13:02 the document object model also. Document
6:13:06 object model is simply the same HTML
6:13:08 document with different representation.
6:13:11 The reason behind this different
6:13:12 representation is that it's easy for
6:13:15 JavaScript to interpret this format.
6:13:18 JavaScript can't understand tags but it
6:13:21 can understand these objects. JavaScript
6:13:24 can easily manipulate these objects
6:13:26 using different functions which we will
6:13:28 see in future while working on these
6:13:30 functions. For example, if you want to
6:13:32 access the text present inside the
6:13:34 heading tag, you can do it by accessing
6:13:37 the heading tag with JavaScript. You can
6:13:39 also work on the style of this text with
6:13:42 the help of JavaScript as well.
6:13:44 JavaScript uses the concept of nodes. It
6:13:47 can access nodes of the document object
6:13:49 model. There are many types of nodes
6:13:52 present in the DOM. The basic three
6:13:54 types are element node, attribute node
6:13:56 and text node. The element node consists
6:13:59 of all the tags used in HTML document
6:14:02 like the head tag and the body tag. The
6:14:04 attribute node contains the attributes
6:14:07 used in HTML document. By now I hope you
6:14:10 have understood the concept of
6:14:11 attributes. So JavaScript can access
6:14:14 these attributes as well and the user
6:14:16 can work upon them as per requirements.
6:14:19 The text here in the HTML document that
6:14:22 is simply it can also be accessed with
6:14:24 the help of JavaScript. It is the value
6:14:27 of that particular element. Variables
6:14:30 are named storage that a program can
6:14:32 manipulate. As the name suggest, a
6:14:34 variable is a value that can change

8:09:48 selector, and CSS group selectors. So

8:09:56 based on the element name. This is a CSS
8:09:59 selector. It means it will select all
8:10:01 the P elements on the page. It will
8:10:03 style all the P elements to the central
8:10:05 aligned having blue color. This is a CSS
8:10:08 selector. It is set to P. This means it
8:10:12 will select all the P elements on the

8:10:36 be unique within the page. So you should
8:10:38 use the ID selector to find a single
8:10:40 unique element. To find an element

8:10:44 character followed by the ID of the
8:10:46 element. So here we have used the hash
8:10:50 paravan. So all the rule will be applied

8:11:09 selector. The class selector finds the
8:11:11 element with a specific class set by the
8:11:13 HTML class attribute. To select an HTML
8:11:16 with a specific class, use a period
8:11:18 character followed by the name of the
8:11:19 class. So this style will be applied to

8:11:33 and color blue. As you can see the
8:11:36 element having the class name class one

8:11:43 The universal selector flips all the

8:11:49 selects all the elements on a

8:11:55 inside our web page will be centrally

8:12:01 are centrally aligned and the colors are

8:12:06 stylesheets, there are often elements
8:12:08 using the same style. To minimize the
8:12:10 code, you can group selectors. To group
8:12:12 the selectors, separate each selectors

8:12:39 this video, we will discuss what is the

8:12:46 the box model better. So let's get
8:12:48 started.
8:12:52 Every element that can be displayed on a
8:12:54 web page is comprised of one or more
8:12:55 rectangular boxes. CSS box model
8:12:58 typically describes how these
8:12:59 rectangular boxes are laid out on a web
8:13:01 page. These boxes can have different
8:13:03 properties and can interact with each
8:13:05 other in different ways. But every box
8:13:07 has a content area and options
8:13:09 surrounding padding, border, and margin
8:13:11 areas.
8:13:13 The following diagram demonstrates how
8:13:15 the content padding, border and margin
8:13:17 CSS properties determine how much space
8:13:19 an element can take on a web page. The
8:13:22 content area consists of content like
8:13:24 text, images or other media. It is
8:13:26 bounded by the content edge and its
8:13:28 dimensions are given by the content box
8:13:30 width and height.
8:13:32 The border area is the area between the
8:13:34 box padding and margin. Its dimensions
8:13:37 are given by the width and height of the
8:13:38 border.
8:13:41 The margin area consists of spaces
8:13:43 between the border and the margin. The
8:13:45 dimensions of the margin area are the
8:13:47 margin box width and margin box height.
8:13:50 It is useful to separate the element
8:13:51 from its neighbors.
8:13:54 The padding area is the area that
8:13:57 includes the element's padding. This
8:13:59 area is actually the space around the
8:14:01 content area and within the border box.
8:14:03 Its dimensions are given by the width of
8:14:05 the padding box and the height of the
8:14:07 padding box.

8:17:37 layouts.
8:17:40 We will start by discussing what is a
8:17:42 grid layout. Then we will move on to the
8:17:45 features of the grid system and some
8:17:47 basic terminologies which will help you
8:17:49 understand the grid system better. In
8:17:52 end we will work on an example to
8:17:54 understand a grid system. So let's get
8:17:57 started.
8:18:01 What is a grid?
8:18:03 A grid is a set of intersecting
8:18:05 horizontal and vertical lines defining
8:18:06 the rows and columns.
8:18:09 And what is a grid layout? So CSS grid
8:18:13 layout is the most powerful layout
8:18:14 system available in CSS. It is a
8:18:17 two-dimensional system meaning it can
8:18:20 handle both columns and rows unlike
8:18:22 flexbox which is largely a
8:18:23 one-dimensional system.
8:18:26 You work with grid layout by applying
8:18:27 the CSS rules to both the parent element
8:18:30 which became the grid container and the
8:18:32 elements children which become the grid
8:18:34 items.
8:18:36 The CSS grid layout excels at dividing a
8:18:38 page into major regions or defining the
8:18:41 relationship in terms of size, position,
8:18:42 and layer between parts of a
8:18:44 controlbuilt HTML primitives.
8:18:47 You can see this is the basic layout of
8:18:49 a grid system. Like tables, grid layout
8:18:53 enables an author to align elements into
8:18:55 columns and rows. However, many more
8:18:58 regular layouts are either possible or
8:19:00 easier with CSS grid than they were with
8:19:02 tables. For example, a grid containers
8:19:05 child element could position themselves.
8:19:08 So they actually overlap and layer
8:19:10 similar to the CSS positioned elements.
8:19:14 So here are the features of the CSS grid
8:19:16 layout. They are fixed and have flexible
8:19:19 track sizes. You can create a grid with
8:19:22 fixed track sizes using pixels. For
8:19:24 example, this sets the grid to the
8:19:26 specified pixel which fits to the layout
8:19:28 you desire. We can also create a grid
8:19:31 using flexible sizes with percentages
8:19:34 with the new FR unit designed for this
8:19:36 purpose.
8:19:39 You can place the items into a precise
8:19:41 location using the grid line numbers,
8:19:43 names or by targeting an area of the
8:19:45 grid. The grid also contains an
8:19:48 algorithm to control the placement of
8:19:50 items not given an explicit position on
8:19:53 the grid.
8:19:56 You can define an explicit grid with a
8:19:58 grid layout. The grid layout
8:20:00 specification is a flexible enough to
8:20:02 add additional rows and columns when
8:20:04 needed. Features such as adding as many
8:20:07 columns that will fit into a container
8:20:08 are included.
8:20:11 It also gives you control of overlapping
8:20:13 content. More than one item can be
8:20:16 placed into a grid cell or area and they
8:20:19 can partially overlap each other. This
8:20:21 layering may then be controlled with the
8:20:23 Z index property.
8:20:27 So here are the basic terminologies you
8:20:29 should know to work with the grid system
8:20:31 better.
8:20:36 The grid line is a dividing line that
8:20:38 make up the structure of the grid. They
8:20:40 can either be vertical or horizontal and
8:20:43 resides on either side of rows and
8:20:44 columns.
8:20:46 A grid column is a space between two
8:20:48 adjacent vertical grid lines. The size
8:20:50 of a grid column is determined by a grid
8:20:52 template columns property. On the other
8:20:55 hand, a grid row is a space between two
8:20:58 adjacent horizontal grid lines. Its size
8:21:01 is determined by a grid template rows
8:21:03 property.
8:21:05 The grid cell is the single unit of a
8:21:07 CSS grid.
8:21:10 And in grid design, a gutter is
8:21:12 basically the width of the space between
8:21:14 columns. A gutter margin is the white
8:21:16 space formed by the adjoining inside
8:21:18 margins of two facing pages in a book or
8:21:21 a space between columns on a page.
8:21:25 So with this let's now move on to the
8:21:28 hands-on demo and see how the grid
8:21:30 system works in actual world. So we are

10:04:00 also covered the differences between
10:04:02 these tags. Apart from all the
10:04:04 differences these tags have, they are
10:04:06 still similar in one aspect. That is
10:04:09 these tags are used to change the
10:04:11 appearance of a web page. Whenever we
10:04:13 use these tags, we change the look and
10:04:16 the feel of a web page. Now, the meta
10:04:20 tag is a bit different from these tags.
10:04:22 This tag does not impact the appearance
10:04:24 of a web page. It is used in the head
10:04:26 section of the HTML document and does
10:04:29 not require any closing tag to work.
10:04:32 What this tag does is it give
10:04:34 information about the data we have on a
10:04:37 web page. Meta refers to metadata.
10:04:40 Metadata means data about data. For
10:04:44 example, if we have a website that
10:04:47 provides the user with some data, it can
10:04:49 be any kind of data and the user can go
10:04:52 through it to gain some information. But
10:04:54 the information about the data present
10:04:56 on a website is given by meta data only.
10:05:00 What does it mean to provide data about
10:05:03 data? For example, if you go to Google
10:05:06 and search for a keyword. Now how Google
10:05:08 works is it goes through the keywords
10:05:11 present in any particular website and
10:05:13 then shows the user that particular
10:05:15 website. Those keywords here refer to
10:05:18 meta data and we have to put them inside
10:05:21 meta tags in HTML. The description we
10:05:24 get while searching for a website is
10:05:26 also present inside the meta tag of any
10:05:28 HTML document.
10:05:30 Basically what we are trying to say is
10:05:32 this tag is used to give essential
10:05:34 information about any particular web
10:05:36 page.
10:05:37 Meta elements can be used to include
10:05:39 name value pairs describing properties
10:05:41 of HTML documents. For example, if you
10:05:44 want to put any keyword for a web page,
10:05:47 the name attribute inside the meta tag
10:05:49 will contain the keyword and the value
10:05:51 attribute will contain the actual
10:05:53 keyword name.
10:05:55 There's no closing tag needed in meta
10:05:57 elements because these elements contain
10:05:59 information within attributes only. We
10:06:02 can use multiple meta tags for different
10:06:04 information. An interactive web page
10:06:07 cannot be created with just this
10:06:09 structure. So you will employ adding
10:06:11 technologies like JavaScript and CSS to
10:06:14 bring interaction and beauty to your
10:06:17 HTML respectively.
10:06:19 That's where CSS comes into picture.
10:06:22 Making web page pages attractive is made
10:06:26 easier with the help of CSS often known
10:06:29 as cascading stylesheets.
10:06:32 The style and experience of a web page
10:06:35 are handled by CSS. The color of the
10:06:37 text, the font style, the spacing
10:06:39 between paragraphs, the size and
10:06:42 arrangement of columns, the background
10:06:44 pictures or colors used, layout designs,
10:06:47 differences in display for various
10:06:48 devices and screen sizes, and a variety
10:06:51 of other effects can all be controlled
10:06:53 using one single language, which is CSS.
10:06:57 Although CSS is simple to grasp and
10:07:00 learn, it offers strong control over how
10:07:02 an HTML document is presented to the

10:21:30 also look at an examples to how to work
10:21:32 with CSS selectors. So what are CSS
10:21:35 selectors?
10:21:37 CSS selectors allow you to select and
10:21:39 style HTML elements. Selectors are used
10:21:42 to find elements based on their ID,
10:21:44 classes, types, attributes, value of
10:21:46 attributes and much more. Let's discuss
10:21:48 the most common selectors. There are
10:21:50 several different types of CSS
10:21:51 selectors. The most common of them are
10:21:53 CSS element selectors, CSS ID selector,
10:21:56 CSS class selector, CSS universal
10:21:59 selector and CSS group selectors. So
10:22:02 what is a CSS element selector?
10:22:04 The element selector selects element
10:22:06 based on the element name. This is a CSS
10:22:09 selector. It means it will select all
10:22:11 the P elements on the page. It will
10:22:13 style all the P elements to the central
10:22:15 aligned having blue color. This is a CSS
10:22:19 selector. It is set to P. This means it
10:22:22 will select all the P elements on the
10:22:24 page. It will style all the P elements
10:22:26 to be center aligned having blue color.
10:22:28 Let's look at what it looks on a page.
10:22:32 So as you can see all the elements are
10:22:34 centrally aligned with a text color
10:22:36 blue. CSS ID selector. The ID selector
10:22:41 uses the ID attribute of an HTML element
10:22:43 to find a specific element. An ID should
10:22:46 be unique within the page. So you should
10:22:48 use the ID selector to find a single
10:22:50 unique element. To find an element
10:22:52 within a specific ID, use a hash
10:22:54 character followed by the ID of the
10:22:56 element. So here we have used the hash
10:23:00 par one. So all the rule will be applied
10:23:02 to the HTML element with ID equal to par
10:23:05 1. So this is an ID selector. It will be
10:23:08 applied to all the HTML element with ID
10:23:10 is equal to par 1. As you can see, it is
10:23:12 applied to the element having the ID
10:23:14 paravan and all the other elements are
10:23:17 defaultly styled. CSS class selector.
10:23:20 The class selector finds the element
10:23:22 with a specific class set by the HTML
10:23:24 class attribute. To select an HTML with
10:23:26 a specific class, use a period character
10:23:28 followed by the name of the class. So,
10:23:30 this style will be applied to all the
10:23:32 elements having the class name class
10:23:34 one. This is a class selector. It is
10:23:36 denoted by a period symbol. All the
10:23:39 elements having a class named class one
10:23:41 will be styled with centrally aligned
10:23:43 and color blue. As you can see the
10:23:46 element having the class name class one
10:23:48 is styled and all the other elements are
10:23:49 set to default. CSS universal selector.
10:23:53 The universal selector selects all the
10:23:55 elements on a page. The universal
10:23:56 selector is assigned by a star
10:23:58 character. The universal selector
10:24:00 selects all the elements on a
10:24:01 stylesheet. As you can see it is denoted
10:24:03 by a star mark. All the elements inside
10:24:05 our web page will be centrally aligned
10:24:08 and the color will be set to blue. As
10:24:10 you can see, all the elements are
10:24:12 centrally aligned and the colors are set
10:24:13 to blue. CSS group selector. In
10:24:16 stylesheets, there are often elements
10:24:18 using the same style. To minimize the
10:24:20 code, you can group selectors. To group
10:24:22 the selectors, separate each selectors
10:24:24 with a comma. Here we have grouped
10:24:26 selectors for H1, H2, and P. So, style
10:24:29 is applied to H1, H2, and P elements
10:24:31 only. It is better to group the
10:24:33 selectors to minimize the code. Here we
10:24:36 have grouped selectors P, H2 and H3. So
10:24:39 style will be applied to only elements
10:24:40 having P, H2 and H3. As you can see, so
10:24:44 all the element of P, H2 and H3 are
10:24:47 centrally aligned and colored to red.
10:24:50 Every element that can be displayed on a
10:24:52 web page is comprised of one or more
10:24:54 rectangular boxes. CSS box model
10:24:56 typically describes how these
10:24:57 rectangular boxes are laid out on a web
10:24:59 page.
10:25:01 These boxes can have different
10:25:02 properties and can interact with each
10:25:03 other in different ways. But every box
10:25:06 has a content area and optional
10:25:07 surrounding padding, border and margin
10:25:09 areas.
10:25:12 The following diagram demonstrates how
10:25:13 the content padding, border and margin
10:25:16 CSS properties determine how much space
10:25:18 an element can take on a web page. The
10:25:21 content area consists of content like
10:25:22 text, images or other media. It is
10:25:25 bounded by the content edge and its
10:25:27 dimensions are given by the content box,
10:25:28 width and height.
10:25:31 The border area is the area between the
10:25:32 box padding and margin. Its dimensions
10:25:35 are given by the width and height of the
10:25:37 border.
10:25:39 The margin area consists of spaces
10:25:41 between the border and the margin. The
10:25:43 dimensions of the margin area are the
10:25:45 margin box width and margin box height.
10:25:48 It is useful to separate the element
10:25:50 from its neighbors.
10:25:53 The padding area is the area that
10:25:56 includes the element's padding. This
10:25:58 area is actually the space around the
10:25:59 content area and within the border box.
10:26:02 Its dimensions are given by the width of
10:26:04 the padding box and the height of the
10:26:05 padding box.

10:29:32 What is a flexbox?
10:29:34 Flexbox also called flexible box model
10:29:37 is basically a layout mode or model that
10:29:40 provides an easy and clean way to
10:29:41 arrange the items within a container.
10:29:44 If you are using a CSS for a while, you
10:29:47 must have used the old block model where
10:29:49 you assign width in terms of percentage
10:29:51 or fixed width and then use the float to
10:29:54 arrange the items on the page. Suppose
10:29:57 for example you want to arrange three
10:29:59 boxes in a row. Then you have to enter
10:30:02 everything manually from width, height
10:30:04 and to padding. Flexbox can do all these
10:30:06 things with ease.
10:30:09 Some of the concepts of flexible box
10:30:11 model is the ability to alter the item
10:30:13 width and height to best fit the
10:30:15 containers available in the free space.
10:30:19 Flex model is direction agnostic which
10:30:22 is different from the box model which is
10:30:24 vertically biased and the inline which
10:30:26 is horizontally biased. Flexbox works
10:30:28 for both.
10:30:31 Flexbox is more effective in a small
10:30:33 scale layouts.
10:30:35 So here are the main properties of the
10:30:37 flex.

10:36:22 What is a grid?
10:36:24 A grid is a set of intersecting
10:36:26 horizontal and vertical lines defining
10:36:28 the rows and columns.
10:36:30 And what is a grid layout? So CSS grid
10:36:34 layout is the most popular layout system
10:36:36 available in CSS. It is a
10:36:38 two-dimensional system meaning it can
10:36:41 handle both columns and rows unlike
10:36:43 flexbox which is largely a
10:36:45 one-dimensional system.
10:36:47 You work with grid layout by applying
10:36:49 the CSS rules to both the parent element
10:36:51 which become the grid container and the
10:36:53 elements children which become the grid
10:36:55 items.
10:36:57 The CSS grid layout excels at dividing a
10:37:00 page into major regions or defining the
10:37:02 relationship in terms of size, position
10:37:04 and layer between parts of a
10:37:06 controlbuilt HTML primitives.
10:37:08 You can see this is a basic layout of a
10:37:10 grid system. like tables. Grid layout
10:37:14 enables an author to align elements into
10:37:16 columns and rows. However, many more
10:37:19 regular layouts are either possible or
10:37:21 easier with CSS grid than they were with
10:37:23 tables. For example, a grid containers
10:37:27 child element could position themselves.
10:37:29 So, they actually overlap and layer
10:37:31 similar to the CSS positioned elements.
10:37:34 So, here are the features of the CSS
10:37:36 grid layout. They are fixed and have
10:37:38 flexible track sizes. You can create a
10:37:41 grid with fixed track sizes using
10:37:43 pixels. For example, this sets the grid
10:37:46 to the specified pixel which fits to the
10:37:48 layout you desire. You can also create a
10:37:51 grid using flexible sizes with
10:37:52 percentages with the new FR unit
10:37:55 designed for this purpose.
10:37:59 You can place the items into a precise
10:38:01 location using the grid line numbers,
10:38:03 names or by targeting an area of the
10:38:05 grid.
10:38:06 The grid also contains an algorithm to
10:38:09 control the placement of items not given
10:38:12 an explicit position on the grid.
10:38:16 You can define an explicit grid with a
10:38:18 grid layout. The grid layer
10:38:20 specification is a flexible enough to
10:38:22 add additional rows and columns when
10:38:24 needed. Features such as adding as many
10:38:27 columns that will fit into a container
10:38:28 are included.
10:38:31 It also gives you control of overlapping
10:38:33 content. More than one item can be
10:38:35 placed into a grid cell or area and they
10:38:39 can partially overlap each other. This
10:38:41 layering may then be controlled with the
10:38:43 Z index property.
10:38:45 So here are the basic terminologies you
10:38:48 should know to work with the grid system
10:38:50 better.
10:38:53 The grid line is a dividing line that
10:38:56 make up the structure of the grid. They
10:38:58 can either be vertical or horizontal and
10:39:00 resides on either side of rows and
10:39:02 columns.
10:39:04 A grid column is a space between two
10:39:06 adjacent vertical grid lines. The size
10:39:08 of a grid column is determined by the
10:39:10 grid template columns property. On the
10:39:13 other hand, a grid row is a space
10:39:15 between two adjacent horizontal grid
10:39:17 lines. Its size is determined by a grid
10:39:20 template rows property.
10:39:23 The grid cell is the single unit of a
10:39:24 CSS grid.
10:39:28 And in grid design, a gutter is
10:39:29 basically the width of the space between
10:39:31 columns. A gutter margin is this white
10:39:33 space formed by the adjoining inside
10:39:36 margins of two facing pages in a book or
10:39:39 a space between columns on a page. So
10:39:42 with this, let's now move on to the
10:39:44 hands-on demo and see how the grid
10:39:46 system works in actual world.

10:39:50 the battle, what if you could do that

11:12:34 stands for application programming
11:12:36 interface to understand API better.

11:13:04 HTML document in a hierarchical form to
11:13:07 make it understandable for JavaScript so

11:13:12 over it. It represents the entire

11:13:21 anything stored in the document. The
11:13:23 entire page content can be accessed or
11:13:25 manipulated by JavaScript. DOM is not
11:13:28 used with JavaScript only. Other
11:13:30 languages like Python also uses DOM. DOM
11:13:34 was designed to be used independently.

13:00:28 an idea there are three types of

13:00:34 the component directives.
13:00:36 Only one component can be instantiated
13:00:39 per element in a template. Now
13:00:41 components are typically custom HTML
13:00:43 elements and each of these elements can

13:08:01 decorator metadata. Now, as mentioned

13:08:46 time being, let's change it to say n
13:08:50 component.
13:08:52 All right. Now when you save it and look
13:08:55 at the browser, the message is not
13:08:58 displayed. So what we can do is you can
13:09:00 copy this
13:09:02 selector and then include this here.
13:09:11 And now you can see that the same
13:09:14 component has been rendered onto your
13:09:16 output. All right. So the selector plays
13:09:19 a crucial role in that. Next up we have
13:09:22 the template. Now a template is an
13:09:25 inline defined template for the view.
13:09:27 Now the template can be used to provide
13:09:29 some particular markup. The markup could
13:09:32 typically include some headings or
13:09:34 paragraphs that are displayed on the UI.
13:09:37 Next up we have template URL. Now it is
13:09:40 the URL for the external file containing
13:09:42 the template for the view. So here you
13:09:45 can see this is basically the external
13:09:48 file that contains the template for the
13:09:50 view. Next up you have styles. Now these
13:09:53 are also inline defined styles that can
13:09:55 be applied to the components view. So
13:09:58 any code that you provide in the CSS
13:10:00 file can also be included here. Next you
13:10:03 have the style URLs. Now this is a list
13:10:06 of URLs to your stylesheets that are
13:10:08 applied on your components view. Next up
13:10:11 you have providers. Now this is again an
13:10:13 array where certain services can be
13:10:15 registered for your component.
13:10:18 Lastly we have animations. As the name
13:10:20 suggests, these are animations that can
13:10:22 be listed for your components. First,
13:10:25 we'll understand what exactly dependency
13:10:27 injection is. Then, we'll understand the
13:10:30 demerit or the drawback of not using
13:10:32 dependency injection.
13:10:34 Then, we'll understand dependency
13:10:36 injection as a design pattern. And
13:10:38 finally, I'll help you understand the
13:10:40 entire concept with the help of a demo.
13:10:43 So, let's begin. What exactly is
13:10:46 dependency injection? So, let's consider
13:10:48 this. I'm sure you're aware that Angular
13:10:50 uses the concept of components. The
13:10:53 entire UI of the application is divided
13:10:56 into several components. Now, what if
13:10:58 all of these components perform similar
13:11:01 tasks? Be it accessing the database,
13:11:03 rendering images, etc. Now, instead of
13:11:06 writing the same piece of code for every
13:11:08 component, you could write the code once
13:11:10 and then perhaps inject the same code
13:11:13 into every component. Now this is
13:11:15 supported by dependency injection. Now
13:11:18 to give you a better example, consider
13:11:21 two classes A and B. Now let's assume
13:11:23 that A uses the objects of class B. And
13:11:27 normally in object- oriented
13:11:28 programming, an instance of class B is
13:11:31 created so that A can access his
13:11:33 objects. But using dependency injection,
13:11:36 we move the creation and binding of
13:11:38 dependent objects outside of the class
13:11:40 that depend on them. So dependency
13:11:42 injection or DI keeps the code more
13:11:45 flexible, testable and mutable. Also
13:11:48 classes can inherit external logic
13:11:50 without having to create on its own. And
13:11:53 lastly, DI benefits directives, pipes,
13:11:55 and components.
13:11:58 Next up, let's have a look at the
13:12:00 drawback of not using dependency
13:12:02 injection. Now let's consider class
13:12:05 postal details that is dependent on the
13:12:08 number and the address class.
13:12:11 In the postal details class, the
13:12:13 constructor creates copies of the number
13:12:15 and address. So when you instantiate a
13:12:18 new postal details class, the
13:12:20 constructor instantiates a unique number
13:12:23 and address. Now although this looks
13:12:25 simple, there's a problem with this
13:12:27 code. Let's assume that the number and
13:12:29 address class constructors now accept
13:12:31 parameters. When we change the numbers
13:12:34 class, the postal details class gets
13:12:36 broken. Now to overcome this, what we
13:12:39 need to do is we need to pass parameter
13:12:41 to the number constructor. So this
13:12:44 applies to address as well. The first
13:12:47 drawback of using this traditional
13:12:48 method is that the code is not very
13:12:51 flexible. Anytime the dependencies
13:12:54 change, the postal details class needs
13:12:56 to be changed as well. And the second
13:12:59 drawback is that the code is not
13:13:00 suitable for testing.
13:13:03 Once you instantiate the new postal
13:13:05 details class, you get the same number
13:13:07 and address. Even if you change the
13:13:09 number and address classes every time,
13:13:11 what if these classes are in turn
13:13:13 dependent on other classes? Then it's
13:13:16 going to be a chain reaction. So to
13:13:17 overcome this, we make use of dependency
13:13:20 injection.
13:13:21 Now let's look at DI as a pattern.
13:13:25 Dependency injection as you all know is
13:13:27 a coding pattern where a class receives
13:13:30 its dependencies from an external source
13:13:32 rather than creating them on its own. So
13:13:34 here in the above example we have moved
13:13:37 the definition of dependencies from
13:13:39 inside the constructor to the
13:13:41 constructor's parameters. So the postal
13:13:44 details class doesn't create the
13:13:46 dependencies anymore. It just consumes
13:13:49 them. So the postal dependencies class
13:13:51 doesn't create the dependencies anymore.
13:13:55 So with that we overcome the drawbacks
13:13:57 of not using DI.
13:13:59 So now that you know what exactly
13:14:01 dependency injection is and how vital it
13:14:03 is. Let's look at a simple demonstration
13:14:06 to understand the concept better. Now
13:14:09 services, classes, modules, components,
13:14:12 all of these are benefactors of
13:14:14 dependency injection. So in the demo I'm
13:14:17 going to be showing how dependency
13:14:18 injection can be used with the help of
13:14:20 services and injecting these services
13:14:22 into classes. So the main uh thought
13:14:25 behind this is normally components are
13:14:28 used to ensure just a good user
13:14:30 experience. These components do not
13:14:33 generally execute tasks. So to execute
13:14:36 these tasks we make use of services. So
13:14:39 a component can delegate tasks like
13:14:41 fetching data from the server any
13:14:43 network related issues etc to a
13:14:45 particular service and these services
13:14:48 can be made available to components.
13:14:50 With the same thought what I'm going to

13:24:40 We'll understand what Angular forms are.
13:24:43 Then we'll look at the different types
13:24:45 of form building supported by Angular.
13:24:48 Next up, we'll get acquainted with
13:24:50 features like form control and group
13:24:52 control. And we learn all of this with
13:24:55 the help of a simple demo. So without
13:24:57 further ado, let's begin.
13:25:00 So what exactly are Angular forms? Now
13:25:04 I'm sure every application that you use
13:25:06 comes with certain amount of form
13:25:08 filling, correct? Be it your login
13:25:10 credentials or your sign up forms or any
13:25:14 sort of registration forms etc. So forms
13:25:17 are an integral part of a web
13:25:18 application. You need forms to update
13:25:21 your profile or to enter sensitive
13:25:23 information or also to perform any sort
13:25:26 of data related tasks. So now let's look
13:25:29 at the different types of form building
13:25:31 supported by Angular.
13:25:34 First up, Angular supports two types of
13:25:37 form building. First you have the
13:25:40 template driven approach and second you
13:25:42 have the reactive approach. Now let's
13:25:44 look at them one by one. So talking
13:25:47 about templated driven approach in this
13:25:49 method the conventional form tag is used
13:25:52 to create forms. So angular
13:25:54 automatically interprets and creates a
13:25:57 form object with the help of the
13:25:59 directives.
13:26:01 Now controls can be added to the form
13:26:03 using the ngmodule tag. Now multiple
13:26:06 controls can be grouped using the ng
13:26:08 control group module. So it basically
13:26:10 uses several module classes to provide
13:26:13 controls. Now, if you don't get this,
13:26:15 don't worry. You'll understand better
13:26:16 when I'm showing you the demo.
13:26:19 Next up is JSON values. So, form value
13:26:22 can be generated using the form dovalue
13:26:24 object. Form data is exported as JSON
13:26:28 values when the submit method is called.
13:26:32 Lastly, basic HTML validations can be
13:26:34 used to validate the form fields. In the
13:26:37 case of custom validations, directives
13:26:39 can be used. So arguably this method is
13:26:42 the simplest way to create an angular
13:26:44 form. So moving on to reactive approach.
13:26:47 Now this approach is the programming
13:26:49 paradigm revolving around data flows and
13:26:52 propagation of change. Now with reactive
13:26:55 forms the component directly manages the
13:26:58 data flows between the form controls and
13:27:00 the data models.
13:27:02 Now reactive forms are coddriven unlike
13:27:05 template driven approach.
13:27:08 Reactive forms eliminate the anti
13:27:10 pattern of updating the data model via
13:27:12 two-way data binding. So it does not use
13:27:15 two-way data binding.
13:27:17 And typically a reactive form control
13:27:20 creation is synchronous and can be unit
13:27:22 tested with synchronous programming
13:27:24 techniques.
13:27:26 So now let's go ahead and look at form
13:27:29 control and form group.
13:27:35 So consider a typical form consisting of
13:27:38 fields like name, age and location. Now
13:27:41 a form control is basically a class that
13:27:44 enables validation of these fields. For
13:27:47 each input field, an instance of this
13:27:49 class is created. Now these instances
13:27:52 help check the values of the field and
13:27:54 see if they're either touched,
13:27:56 untouched, dirty, pristine, valid,
13:27:59 invalid, and so on. So this allows you
13:28:01 to check every field for the value. It
13:28:04 makes it easier to understand which
13:28:05 fields value is invalid or which field
13:28:08 has not been entered etc.
13:28:12 Now moving on to form group.
13:28:15 So a form group class represents a group
13:28:17 of controls. A form can have multiple
13:28:20 group controls. The form group returns
13:28:23 true if all the controls are valid and
13:28:26 provides validation errors if there are
13:28:28 any. So I hope this was clear. So moving
13:28:31 ahead, let's look at the demo to explain
13:28:33 all of these features. I'm sure it will
13:28:35 become extremely clear once you look at
13:28:37 the demo. So for that, let's head to our
13:28:40 VS code. So back in my VS code, I've
13:28:44 created a component called form

13:44:32 First let's have a look at the basics of
13:44:35 routing. Now I'm sure that you observed
13:44:38 that the URL changes every time you
13:44:40 click on a particular link or on an icon
13:44:43 on the user interface. Correct? Now this
13:44:46 basically indicates that you are
13:44:48 navigating throughout the application.
13:44:50 Now to achieve this navigation we use a
13:44:53 mechanism called routing. Routing is
13:44:56 essentially used to navigate through a
13:44:58 website or a web application.
13:45:02 But how is it achieved in Angular?
13:45:05 Especially in Angular routing plays a
13:45:07 major role. It is used to create single
13:45:10 page applications. Now these
13:45:12 applications are loaded just once and
13:45:15 new content is added dynamically.
13:45:18 Applications like Google, Facebook,
13:45:20 Twitter and Gmail are a few examples of
13:45:23 single page applications. The best part
13:45:26 of using single page applications is
13:45:28 that it provides an excellent user
13:45:30 experience and you don't even have to
13:45:32 wait for new pages to load which by
13:45:35 extension makes single page applications
13:45:37 extremely fast. But how is this new
13:45:40 content loaded?
13:45:42 Enter routing. Now routing is the
13:45:45 mechanism that as discussed earlier that
13:45:47 is used to navigate through the
13:45:49 application and load new content.
13:45:52 Routing helps Angular configure the
13:45:54 route and the component that needs to be
13:45:56 displayed.
13:45:58 Now I'm sure you're going to understand
13:45:59 the concept of routing better when you
13:46:02 work on it hands-on. So in the demo,
13:46:04 we're going to be navigating to the
13:46:06 login and the homepages of a web
13:46:08 application with the help of routing. So
13:46:11 let's head to our Visual Studio Code. So

13:46:16 application is to navigate to home and

13:57:27 Let's go ahead and begin with what is
13:57:30 React. React is a JavaScript library
13:57:32 used to build fast and interactive user
13:57:34 interfaces for both web and mobile
13:57:37 applications. Now, one thing that makes
13:57:39 React extremely powerful is the fact
13:57:41 that it's open source. Any user can
13:57:43 access its source code, modify it, and
13:57:46 enhance it. In a typical model view
13:57:48 controller architecture, React defines
13:57:50 the view part of the application. It is
13:57:52 also responsible for how the app looks
13:57:54 and feels. Fun fact, React was built by
13:57:57 Jordan Walke, an engineer at Facebook.
13:57:59 Now, let's go ahead and see how React
13:58:01 works in real time. Consider a typical
13:58:04 web application, say Instagram. The
13:58:06 entire UI of the web page is divided
13:58:08 into several components. We have the
13:58:10 search component, a profile description
13:58:12 component, stories component, and a post
13:58:15 list component. These components make
13:58:17 the code easier to debug and always
13:58:20 remain discreet. Components also make
13:58:22 development and maintenance of web
13:58:24 applications faster as multiple
13:58:26 developers can work simultaneously on
13:58:28 different components of the same web
13:58:30 application. Now let's see why React is
13:58:32 so popular these days. DAC helps in
13:58:35 creation of dynamic web applications. It
13:58:37 also provides performance enhancements.
13:58:39 Now there are several performance
13:58:41 enhancements like virtual DOM and oneway
13:58:43 data binding. We'll learn about these
13:58:45 topics a little later in the video.
13:58:46 React uses reusable components. Now this
13:58:49 helps decrease the development time.
13:58:51 React involves unidirectional flow of
13:58:54 data. React also has a small learning
13:58:56 curve. Compared to other frameworks like
13:58:58 Angular, React is much easier to learn.
13:59:00 React can also be used for mobile
13:59:02 applications. React recently released an
13:59:05 extension called React Native that is
13:59:07 used for developing mobile applications
13:59:09 that are cross-co compatible. It also
13:59:11 has dedicated tools for easy debugging.
13:59:13 Now let's dive in to the features of
13:59:15 React. First let's understand JSX. JSX
13:59:19 is a syntax extension to JavaScript.
13:59:22 Using JSX we can add HTML to the
13:59:24 JavaScript file. All coding in React is
13:59:27 done using JSX. It also helps in making
13:59:30 the code easier to understand and debug.
13:59:33 Ultimately, JSX is a combination of
13:59:36 JavaScript and HTML. Now, let's consider
13:59:39 a small example. Con simple here is a

13:59:44 HTML. Again, the semicolon indicates
13:59:47 JavaScript. Note that the semicolon is
13:59:50 not mandatory. However, it's a good
13:59:52 practice to include it. Moving on, the
13:59:54 next feature is virtual DOM. DOM which
13:59:57 is an acronym for data object model
13:59:59 actually defines how documents are
14:00:01 accessed and manipulated in a web page.
14:00:04 It represents the entire structure of
14:00:06 the web page in the form of a tree. Now
14:00:09 let's see how this actually works. React
14:00:12 creates a virtual DOM that is the exact
14:00:14 copy of the real DOM. Traditionally
14:00:17 whenever something changes in the web
14:00:19 application all objects in the real DOM
14:00:21 are updated. This makes it extremely
14:00:24 slow. Let's see how React counters this.
14:00:26 Now consider two objects whose states
14:00:29 have been changed. React now compares
14:00:31 the virtual DOM with the virtual DOM
14:00:33 snapshot that was taken right before the
14:00:35 update. By comparing the new virtual DOM
14:00:37 with the pre-update version, React
14:00:39 figures out exactly which virtual DOM
14:00:41 objects have changed. Now React only
14:00:44 updates those objects on the virtual
14:00:46 DOM. This makes a big difference when it
14:00:48 comes to speed. Coming to the next
14:00:50 feature, performance. React uses virtual
14:00:53 DOM. As discussed earlier, virtual DOM
14:00:56 impacts the speed of the web
14:00:57 applications. We also took an example of
14:00:59 Instagram and learned how multiple
14:01:01 components can affect the development
14:01:03 time. They help improve performance
14:01:05 drastically. The next feature is oneway
14:01:08 data binding. This means information
14:01:10 flows in only one direction. Oneway data
14:01:12 binding is specifically used when
14:01:14 information is displayed and not
14:01:16 updated. Remember that the components of
14:01:18 React are functional in nature. that is
14:01:20 they receive information through
14:01:22 arguments and pass information via their
14:01:24 return values. The next important
14:01:26 feature is extensions. React has many
14:01:29 extensions that we can use to create
14:01:31 full-fledged front-end applications. It
14:01:33 provides serverside rendering which
14:01:35 means that the application is rendered
14:01:37 on the server rather than in the
14:01:39 browser. React Native which is a React
14:01:41 extension is used for developing mobile
14:01:43 applications. There are many other
14:01:45 extensions that React provides for every
14:01:47 scenario in web development. Now let's
14:01:49 look at a few of them. React Native lets
14:01:52 you build mobile applications using only
14:01:54 JavaScript. A React Native app is a real
14:01:56 mobile application and not just a web
14:01:58 application running on your mobile
14:02:00 devices. Thousands of applications like
14:02:03 Facebook, Instagram, Pinterest, Skype
14:02:05 use React Native. The next popular React
14:02:08 extension is Flux. Flux is the
14:02:10 application architecture that Facebook
14:02:12 uses. Flux implements a unidirectional
14:02:15 flow which makes it easier to understand
14:02:17 what actually is going on. Now let's see
14:02:19 how it works. When we trigger an action,
14:02:22 the dispatcher will get notified. The
14:02:24 dispatcher receives actions and
14:02:26 dispatches them to the stores. A store
14:02:28 is what holds the data of an
14:02:30 application. Stores will register with
14:02:32 the application's dispatcher so that
14:02:34 they can receive actions. Data from
14:02:36 stores is displayed in views. Actions
14:02:38 define the internal API of your
14:02:40 application. They capture how anything
14:02:42 might interact with your application.
14:02:44 The last feature of React is debugging.
14:02:47 React applications are easy to debug

14:03:05 let's learn about the building blocks of

14:03:14 the foundation on which React is built.

14:03:21 any React application. React divides the
14:03:23 user interface into multiple components

14:03:36 reusability. Components can be reused
14:03:39 multiple times across the application.
14:03:41 This reduces the development time.
14:03:44 Consider the following code snippet. A
14:03:46 component is implemented as a JavaScript
14:03:48 class having some state and a render
14:03:50 method. State is the data which we want
14:03:53 the component to render. The render
14:03:55 method is responsible for how the UI
14:03:57 looks and feels to the user. The next
14:04:00 concept is state. State of a component
14:04:02 is an object that holds some data. This
14:04:05 data influences the output of a
14:04:07 component. Every time the state of an
14:04:09 object changes, the component is
14:04:11 rerendered onto the screen. Now, let's
14:04:13 see what props are. Props or short for
14:04:16 properties allow us to pass arguments or
14:04:18 data to components. Properties help make

14:04:23 to define all the properties, their

14:23:38 does not implement the should component

14:23:42 this method a little later in the video.

14:23:46 components to the DOM. But a pure
14:23:48 component on the other hand ensures a
14:23:49 shallow comparison of states and props
14:23:52 and checks if a rerender is actually
14:23:54 necessary. Only then does it render to
14:23:56 the DOM. A react component can be
14:23:58 considered pure if it renders the same
14:24:00 output for the same state and props. So

14:28:01 component life cycle. So basically
14:28:03 component life cycle explains the stages
14:28:06 the component goes through. The entire
14:28:08 life cycle of a component can be divided
14:28:10 into three parts. We have mounting,
14:28:12 updation and unmounting. So the first
14:28:14 thing that gets invoked in the life
14:28:16 cycle is component will mount. It is
14:28:18 invoked right before the initial
14:28:20 rendering occurs. Then we have the
14:28:22 rendering method. Now once a component
14:28:24 is inserted into the DOM, component did
14:28:26 mount gets invoked. Coming to the next
14:28:28 phase, we have updation. When a
14:28:29 component receives props, component will
14:28:32 receive props gets invoked. Set state is
14:28:34 called when the state of the property
14:28:36 changes. So every time there is a
14:28:38 change, React decides whether the next
14:28:40 component's state should trigger a
14:28:42 rerender or not. To do that, should
14:28:44 component update method is called.
14:28:46 Component will update is invoked right
14:28:48 before rerendering. After rerendering,
14:28:51 component did update is called that
14:28:53 makes updates to the DOM. And the last
14:28:55 phase is unmounting. Component will
14:28:57 unmount method does all the necessary
14:28:59 cleanup and erases all the allocated
14:29:01 memory and cycles. In our previous
14:29:03 video, we learned about ReactJS
14:29:06 components in detail. And now moving

14:29:09 striking feature of ReactJS called

14:29:17 to components. A pairing component can

14:29:21 children using props. Properties help

14:29:27 passed to components in the way similar
14:29:29 to that of HTML track attributes. Now,
14:29:31 we'll look at this a little later in the

14:29:35 only and cannot be changed. One thing to
14:29:37 remember is that props are sent by the
14:29:39 parent to the children components.
14:29:41 Hence, the children components cannot
14:29:43 make any changes to these props. Now

14:36:01 Let me save it. And now if you look at a
14:36:03 browser, here we go. We get the output
14:36:05 as expected. So let's begin and learn
14:36:08 what exactly is a state in React.
14:36:11 Typically a state is an object that
14:36:13 stores the values of properties
14:36:15 belonging to a component. Now these
14:36:17 values can change over a period of time
14:36:19 either via user interactions or network
14:36:22 changes and the state helps facilitate
14:36:24 this functionality. Every time the state
14:36:26 changes, React renders the component to
14:36:29 the browser. The state is initialized in
14:36:31 the constructor. A state can also store
14:36:34 multiple properties. A method called set
14:36:36 state is used to update the value of the
14:36:38 state object. Now this function performs
14:36:41 a shallow merge on the new and the
14:36:43 previous state. Conventionally a shallow
14:36:45 merge ensures that the previous state
14:36:47 values are overwritten by the new state
14:36:49 values. Moving on. In our previous
14:36:51 video, we learned about props. And
14:36:53 although props and state dictate and
14:36:55 control the behavior of a component,
14:36:56 they have significant differences. So
14:36:58 let's go ahead and draw a comparison
14:37:00 between the two. Firstly, props in a
14:37:02 component are used to pass data and
14:37:04 event handlers to its children. While
14:37:06 state on the other hand is used to store
14:37:08 the data that has to be rendered on the
14:37:10 web page. Props are immutable. Once set
14:37:12 by the parent, they cannot be changed.
14:37:14 State holds volatile data and can be
14:37:17 changed over time. Props can be used in
14:37:19 functional and class components while
14:37:21 state is restricted to class components.
14:37:23 Props are set by the parent component
14:37:25 while a state is generally updated by
14:37:27 event handlers. Now that we've learned
14:37:29 all about state, let's go ahead and
14:37:31 build an application to see the working
14:37:33 of state. All right. So in my code

14:46:27 So what exactly is NodeJS? Now consider
14:46:30 a scenario where a user interacts with a
14:46:32 web application. The user only interacts
14:46:35 with the front end of the application.
14:46:37 But there's a lot of work that goes
14:46:38 behind the scenes. Now in a web
14:46:40 application, there's a front end, a
14:46:42 server, and a database. The front end is
14:46:45 developed using JavaScript and
14:46:47 frameworks like Angular and React. While
14:46:49 the server is built on Java, PHP or
14:46:51 NodeJS, and the backend database that
14:46:53 stores the data usually consists of
14:46:55 MySQL or MongoDB. So NodeJS is an
14:46:58 open-source cross-platform JavaScript
14:47:00 runtime environment for running
14:47:02 serverside applications. It is used to
14:47:04 develop IO inensive web applications
14:47:07 like video streaming sites, single page
14:47:09 applications, online chatting
14:47:11 applications and other web applications.
14:47:13 It is used by large established
14:47:15 companies and newly minted startups.
14:47:17 NodeJS was developed by Ryan Dal in 2009
14:47:20 and at the same time of this session its
14:47:22 latest version is V13.3.0.
14:47:25 Now that we know what NodeJS is, let's
14:47:27 go ahead and look at why it was so
14:47:29 prevalent in the field of web
14:47:30 development. Here are a few points.
14:47:33 First up, NodeJS is extremely fast.
14:47:35 Being built on Google Chrome's V8
14:47:37 JavaScript engine, its library is
14:47:39 extremely fast in code execution. Node
14:47:42 package manager has around 50,000
14:47:44 bundles for developers. So whatever
14:47:46 functionality is required for an
14:47:47 application can be easily imported from
14:47:49 npm. All APIs of NodeJS library are
14:47:52 asynchronous that is non-blocking. It
14:47:55 means a NodeJS based server never waits
14:47:57 for an API to return data. NodeJS
14:48:00 dramatically reduces the processing time
14:48:01 while uploading video and audio files.
14:48:04 It is also an open-source platform and
14:48:06 is easy for beginners for web
14:48:08 development. The above reasons more than
14:48:10 justify the popularity of NodeJS
14:48:12 platform and why it is being adopted by
14:48:14 large number of organizations and
14:48:16 businesses today. So now moving on let's
14:48:18 familiarize ourselves with the parts of
14:48:20 NodeJS. NodeJS consists of several
14:48:23 components. Now let's look at them one
14:48:25 by one. First up we have modules. Now
14:48:28 modules are like JavaScript libraries
14:48:30 which are used in a NodeJS application
14:48:32 to include a set of functions. Now we
14:48:34 can use require function with the
14:48:36 parenthesis to include a module in a
14:48:38 NodeJS application. NodeJS application
14:48:40 has many modules to provide basic
14:48:42 functionality needed for a web
14:48:44 application. Some of them could be HTTP,
14:48:47 util, FS, URL, query, string, stream,
14:48:50 and zip. Next up, we have console. The
14:48:53 console module provides a simple
14:48:55 debugging console that is similar to the
14:48:57 JavaScript console mechanism provided by
14:48:59 web browsers. Now, the console can print
14:49:01 messages to std out that print standard
14:49:04 output messages and std to print the
14:49:06 error messages. The next component is
14:49:09 cluster. Now, NodeJS is built upon the
14:49:11 concept of single threading programming.
14:49:13 A single instance of NodeJS runs in a
14:49:16 single thread. To take advantage of
14:49:17 multi-core systems, the user will
14:49:19 sometimes want to launch a cluster of
14:49:21 NodeJS processes to handle the load. A
14:49:23 cluster can be added to an application
14:49:25 in this way. Here's a small code for
14:49:27 you. The next component is global. Now,
14:49:30 global objects in NodeJS are available
14:49:32 in all modules. Now, these objects are
14:49:34 functions, modules, strings, etc. Some
14:49:37 NodeJS global objects are mentioned in
14:49:39 the table. You have diir name that is a
14:49:42 directory name, file name, exports,
14:49:44 module and require. The next component
14:49:47 is error handling. Now NodeJS
14:49:49 applications experience four types of
14:49:51 errors. We have the standard JavaScript
14:49:53 errors, system errors, user specific
14:49:56 errors and assertion errors. Now errors
14:49:58 in NodeJS are handled through
14:49:59 exceptions. Now for example, let's
14:50:01 handle an error that would occur when we
14:50:03 divide a number by zero. Now this error
14:50:05 would crash the NodeJS application and
14:50:07 so we should handle this error. Here's a
14:50:09 small code for you. Next up, we have
14:50:12 streaming. A stream is an abstract
14:50:14 interface for working with streaming
14:50:16 data in NodeJS. Streams are objects that
14:50:18 let you read data or write data
14:50:20 continuously. There are four types of
14:50:22 streams. First one is the readable.
14:50:25 These are the types of streams from
14:50:26 which data can be read. Writable, these
14:50:29 are the types of streams to which data
14:50:30 can be written. Duplex, these are both
14:50:33 readable and writable streams. Lastly,
14:50:36 we have transform. These streams can
14:50:38 manipulate the data while it is being
14:50:40 read or written. Moving on to the next
14:50:42 component, buffer. The JavaScript
14:50:44 language has no mechanism for reading or
14:50:46 manipulating streams of binary data.
14:50:49 Buffer is a module that allows handling
14:50:51 streams that contain only binary data.
14:50:53 An empty buffer of length 10 can be
14:50:55 created by this method. Next up, we have
14:50:57 domain. Domains provide a way to handle
14:51:00 different IO operations as a single
14:51:02 group. Domain module intercepts errors
14:51:04 that remain unhandled. There are two
14:51:06 types of methods that are used for
14:51:08 intercepting these errors. Firstly, we
14:51:10 have internal binding. In this case, the
14:51:12 error emitter executes its code inside
14:51:14 the error method. Next up is the
14:51:16 external binding. Now, here the error
14:51:18 emitter is explicitly added to a domain
14:51:20 via its add method. The next component
14:51:23 is the DNS. The DNS module enables name
14:51:26 resolution. For example, it is used to
14:51:28 look up the IP addresses of host names.
14:51:31 DNS module is used to connect to a DNS
14:51:33 server and perform name resolution by
14:51:35 using the method DNS.resolve. The DNS
14:51:38 module is also used for performing name
14:51:40 resolution without a network
14:51:41 communication by using the method
14:51:43 DNS.lookup. The next component is the
14:51:46 debugger. NodeJS includes an out of
14:51:48 process debugging utility accessible via
14:51:50 a V8 inspector and built-in debugging
14:51:52 client. This NodeJS debugger is not
14:51:55 feature- packed but supports simple
14:51:56 inspection of code. It can be used in
14:51:59 the terminal by using the inspect
14:52:00 keyword before the name of the
14:52:02 JavaScript file. In order to inspect a
14:52:04 file, say myScript.js, you can use the
14:52:06 following method. Now that we are
14:52:08 familiar with all the major parts of
14:52:10 NodeJS, let's go ahead and learn about
14:52:12 the NodeJS Express framework. Express is
14:52:15 a flexible NodeJS web application
14:52:17 framework that provides a wide set of
14:52:19 features to develop both web and mobile
14:52:21 applications. You can assume express as
14:52:23 a layer built on top of the NodeJS that
14:52:26 helps manage the server and the roots.
14:52:28 Now let's have a quick look at some of
14:52:30 the core features of Express framework.
14:52:32 It is used to design single page,
14:52:34 multi-page and hybrid web applications.
14:52:36 It allows setting up of middleware to
14:52:38 respond to HTTP requests. It defines a
14:52:40 routing table that is used to perform
14:52:42 different actions based on the HTTP
14:52:44 method and URL. It also allows dynamic
14:52:46 rendering of HTML pages based on passing
14:52:49 arguments to templates. Now moving on
14:52:51 let's take a quick example of a simple
14:52:53 hello world program. Now this is
14:52:55 developed using express framework to
14:52:57 gain a better understanding. All right
14:52:59 first up we have the where express. Now
14:53:01 this is used to import express framework
14:53:03 into our NodeJS application. Now the
14:53:05 app.get method is a callback function

14:53:12 request and has properties for the

15:02:16 >> So let's talk about the Node.js
15:02:18 architecture and understand why it's so
15:02:21 good. So to begin with, NodeJS has
15:02:24 what's called a singlethreaded event
15:02:26 loop architecture. And this allows you
15:02:29 to be able to handle many many
15:02:31 concurrent clients at the same time.
15:02:34 It's just a very flexible way of being
15:02:36 able to scale up your support for many
15:02:40 uh clients.
15:02:42 And then in addition to that, we have
15:02:44 the JavaScript eventbased model along
15:02:47 with the JavaScript callback mechanism
15:02:49 that allows you to have this standard
15:02:52 approach to being able to connect with
15:02:54 and talk and discuss interactions
15:02:57 between the client and the server. It's
15:03:00 good to have these kind of standards
15:03:02 where you're not using something that's
15:03:03 unique to a specific framework or a
15:03:06 specific technology. This just makes it
15:03:08 so it's a more accessible for everybody.
15:03:12 So if we look at the actual NodeJS
15:03:16 server itself, everything centers around
15:03:18 the the concept of the event loop. And
15:03:20 the event loop has a a an event Q an
15:03:24 operation completed um via requests
15:03:27 threaded pull and then it actually do
15:03:30 the actual computation or the file
15:03:31 system interaction.
15:03:33 All of this interconnects in a nice
15:03:36 seamless loop. The great thing again
15:03:38 with NodeJS is that it's not new
15:03:40 technology. It's been around for almost
15:03:41 a decade. It's very very well tried out.
15:03:45 Many of the largest websites out there
15:03:48 are running NodeJS.
15:03:51 So let's now dig into each of the parts
15:03:53 of the NodeJS architecture. So let's
15:03:55 talk about requests.
15:03:58 So the way that the request works is
15:04:00 that you have your framework uh such as
15:04:04 Angular on the client and that will then
15:04:07 send a request to the web server and
15:04:09 there's many ways that those requests
15:04:11 can happen through web workers, through
15:04:13 forms and through other interactions.
15:04:17 But at some point a request is sent to
15:04:19 the server.
15:04:21 And so the these requests can be for
15:04:23 data, they can be for deleting data,
15:04:25 they can be for updating data, they can
15:04:27 be for interacting with services as
15:04:30 mentioned before, but the goal is to
15:04:32 have some kind of request. Now once that
15:04:34 request actually hits the server and
15:04:36 then we start actually getting into the
15:04:38 queueing and threading process that
15:04:40 centers around the event loop and this
15:04:43 is where NodeJS really excels because
15:04:46 it's able to use that single threaded
15:04:48 model to be able to manage from just
15:04:51 even a few requests to tens of millions
15:04:54 of requests.
15:04:56 So it receives the actual uh request and
15:04:58 starts the event quue. And this is where
15:05:00 that single threading process starts is
15:05:03 by queuing everything up appropriately
15:05:06 and then it then maintains the threads
15:05:10 within a pool. So you have the queueing
15:05:12 process as one action and then you have
15:05:15 a threaded pool which manages those um
15:05:18 threads as well. And then we have an
15:05:20 event loop which you know goes through
15:05:22 and actually receives and processes
15:05:24 those requests. And then finally what
15:05:27 we're doing is we're actually then going
15:05:29 to be interacting with a system and that
15:05:31 could be a database it could be a cloud
15:05:34 system. It could be an API file system
15:05:37 but we're interacting with something
15:05:39 that's outside of the NodeJS server. So
15:05:41 the NodeJS server itself is doing some
15:05:43 actions on the server but then it's also
15:05:47 interact with things outside of its own
15:05:49 server environment. And so let's talk a
15:05:52 little bit about that workflow. So
15:05:54 requests come in, they go into the event
15:05:56 Q and they get spiraled through the
15:05:58 event loop and then we have a
15:06:00 non-blocking operators which are going
15:06:02 to be doing the input output polling uh
15:06:04 such as KQ, EOL and other services. And
15:06:08 from there, we're also going to be then
15:06:09 interacting with a threaded pool and
15:06:11 we're using the blocking operations to
15:06:14 manage that threaded pool. And that
15:06:16 threaded pool is going to now then go
15:06:18 outside of the server and interact with

16:55:32 server architecture. SQL when deployed
