16:55:43 features and applications, let's go
16:55:46 through some basic queries in SQL.
16:55:49 That is DDL data definition language,
16:55:52 DML data manipulation language, DCL data
16:55:56 control language, TCL transaction
16:55:58 control language. DDL or data definition
16:56:01 language consists of the SQL commands
16:56:04 that can be used to define the database
16:56:06 schema. Let's see DDL commands. Create
16:56:09 command is used to create the database
16:56:11 or its objects like table, index,
16:56:14 function, views, store procedure, and
16:56:17 triggers. Drop is used to delete objects
16:56:19 from the database. Truncate is used to
16:56:22 remove all records from a table. Alter
16:56:25 is used to alter the structure of the
16:56:27 database.
16:56:29 DML or data manipulation language
16:56:32 consists of the SQL commands that deals
16:56:34 with the manipulation of data present in
16:56:36 the database. Insert into command is
16:56:39 used to insert data into a table. Update
16:56:42 is used to update existing data within a
16:56:44 table. Delete is used to delete records
16:56:47 from a database table. Select this
16:56:49 command helps you to select the
16:56:51 attribute based on the condition
16:56:53 described by the work close.
16:56:56 TCLO transaction control language. These
16:56:59 commands deals with the transaction
16:57:01 within the database. Commit commands
16:57:03 commit a transaction. Roll back command
16:57:06 roll backs a transaction in case of any
16:57:09 error occurs. Save point. It sets a safe
16:57:11 point within a transaction. Set
16:57:14 transaction. It specify characteristics
16:57:16 for the transaction.
16:57:19 DCL or data control language. It
16:57:21 consists of the commands such as grant
16:57:23 and revoke which mainly deals with the
16:57:25 rights, permissions and other controls
16:57:28 of the database system. Grant command
16:57:31 gives user access privileges to
16:57:33 database. revoke it will draw users
16:57:36 access privileges given by using the
16:57:39 grant command. Now you'll have hands on
16:57:41 some basic queries in MySQL workbench.

17:19:52 alphabetically here. So what exactly is
17:19:55 MongoDB?
17:19:57 It is a documentoriented NoSQL database.
17:20:01 It was initially released in August
17:20:03 27th, 2009.
17:20:07 MongoDB replaces the concept of rows of
17:20:10 conventional relational data models with
17:20:12 something called as documents. It offers
17:20:15 developers the flexibility to work with
17:20:17 evolving data models. Since it's
17:20:19 document- based, MongoDB allows embedded
17:20:22 documents, arrays and represents complex
17:20:25 hierarchical relationships using a
17:20:27 single record. It is also schemafree
17:20:30 which means that the keys defined in the
17:20:32 document are not fixed. As a result,
17:20:35 massive data migrations can be ruled
17:20:37 out. But you must be wondering when
17:20:39 there's something like SQL, why is no
17:20:42 SQL or MongoDB used? There are a few
17:20:45 reasons why MongoDB is widely used.
17:20:48 First up is flexibility. MongoDB's
17:20:50 notion of documents that can contain
17:20:53 subdocuments nested in complex
17:20:55 hierarchies is really expressive and
17:20:57 flexible. Flexible query model. A user
17:21:01 can selectively index some part of a
17:21:03 document or a query based on attribute
17:21:06 values, regular expressions or ranges.
17:21:10 Next up is native aggregation. Native
17:21:12 aggregation allows its users to extract
17:21:15 and transform data from MongoDB and
17:21:18 either load them in a new format or
17:21:20 export it from MongoDB to other data
17:21:22 sources. It makes it extremely
17:21:24 compatible.
17:21:26 Lastly, as mentioned earlier, the
17:21:27 schemafree model. Now applications get
17:21:30 the power and responsibility to
17:21:32 interpret different properties in
17:21:34 different ways. Now that you know what
17:21:36 MongoDB is and why exactly it's used,
17:21:39 let's have a look at the characteristics
17:21:41 of MongoDB.
17:21:43 Firstly, it's a general purpose
17:21:44 database. Now, MongoDB can serve
17:21:47 heterogeneous loads and multiple
17:21:49 purposes within an application.
17:21:51 Again, it has a flexible schema design.
17:21:54 Documenc-toriented approaches with
17:21:56 non-defined attributes that can be
17:21:58 modified on the fly is a key contrast
17:22:01 between MongoDB and any other relational
17:22:03 database.
17:22:05 Next up is scalability and load
17:22:07 balancing. It's built to scale both
17:22:09 vertically, but most importantly
17:22:11 horizontally. Using sharding, an
17:22:14 architect can share load between
17:22:16 different instances and achieve both
17:22:18 read and write scalability. When it
17:22:21 comes to load balancing, this happens
17:22:23 automatically and transparently to the
17:22:25 user by the shard balancer.
17:22:28 Again, we have aggregation framework.
17:22:30 Now, MongoDB offers an extract,
17:22:32 transform, and load framework that
17:22:34 eliminates the need for complex data
17:22:37 pipelines.
17:22:39 Native replication. Now, data will get
17:22:42 replicated across a replica set without
17:22:45 complicated setup.
17:22:47 Security features both authentication
17:22:50 and authorization are taken into account
17:22:52 in MongoDB.
17:22:54 Next up is JSON. Now JSON is widely used
17:22:57 across the web for front end and API
17:23:00 communication and as such it's easier
17:23:02 when the database is also compatible
17:23:04 with the same protocol.
17:23:07 Lastly, we have map reduce. Now again
17:23:10 map reduce is an excellent tool to build
17:23:12 data pipelines and MongoDB uses map
17:23:15 reduce readily. Now let's quickly have a
17:23:17 look at the working of MongoDB.
17:23:21 Data is stored in documents with the
17:23:23 help of key value pairs. Now the data is
17:23:26 stored in the BSON format. At the back
17:23:29 end, MongoDB converts JSON data into a
17:23:32 binary format known as BSON. MongoDB
17:23:35 also makes provision for nested data.
17:23:38 Now all of these documents are stored in
17:23:40 a collection. These collections hold
17:23:43 multiple documents and since MongoDB is
17:23:45 schemaless, the documents in one
17:23:47 collection need not be similar. Now
17:23:50 again these collections are stored in
17:23:51 the entire MongoDB database. As
17:23:54 mentioned earlier, MongoDB makes
17:23:56 provision for nested data. This makes
17:23:58 fetching of data extremely efficient

19:22:59 >> Let's understand what is NestJS. Now,
19:23:02 NestJS is a modern framework designed to
19:23:04 help developers to build scalable and
19:23:07 maintainable serverside application
19:23:09 using NodeJS. Now, it is built with
19:23:12 Typescript which is a supererset of
19:23:14 JavaScript that adds static types
19:23:17 ensuring better development tools and
19:23:19 reducing the chance of errors in your
19:23:21 code. But don't worry, if you prefer
19:23:24 JavaScript, you can still use NestJS
19:23:26 with plain JavaScript. At its core,
19:23:29 NestJS wraps around popular libraries
19:23:32 like Express or Fastify to handle HTTP
19:23:35 request while offering a cleaner, more
19:23:38 structured way of building your
19:23:40 application. JS organizes everything
19:23:42 into modules, controllers, and services
19:23:45 to help you manage your code better as
19:23:47 your project grows. First one is
19:23:50 modules. Now modules will help you to
19:23:52 organize related pieces of functionality
19:23:54 together. Next we've got controllers.
19:23:57 Now controllers handle incoming request
19:23:59 and it returns responses. Then we've got
19:24:02 providers or services. Now this contains
19:24:04 business logic such as data manipulation
19:24:07 and database interactions. NodeJS also
19:24:11 makes heavy use of TypeScript type
19:24:13 system which means that the code is more
19:24:16 predictable and safer to use. Now let's
19:24:19 talk about why should you use NestJS in
19:24:22 2026. Now in coming years backend
19:24:25 development is becoming more complex and
19:24:27 developers need frameworks that are
19:24:29 scalable, secure and easy to maintain.
19:24:32 NestJS is perfect for meeting these
19:24:35 needs. Here's why. First of all, it
19:24:37 gives you clear structure and best
19:24:39 practices. NodeJS gives you a structured
19:24:42 and opinated way to build applications
19:24:45 which means you don't have to make every
19:24:47 decision about how to organize your code
19:24:50 from day one. Nestjs provides a standard
19:24:53 layout that works well for both small
19:24:55 and large projects. Then we've got
19:24:58 modularity and scalability. Now as your
19:25:00 project grows, NESJS makes it easy to
19:25:03 break your app into smaller independent
19:25:06 modules like authentication or product
19:25:09 management. Approach allows teams to
19:25:11 work on different parts of the app
19:25:14 without stepping on each other toes
19:25:17 making the code base easier to scale and
19:25:19 maintain. Then we've got dependency
19:25:21 injection DI made easy. NSJS uses
19:25:25 dependency injection to manage how
19:25:27 pieces of your application communicate
19:25:29 with each other. This makes your code
19:25:32 loosely coupled, meaning that parts of
19:25:34 your code are less dependent on each
19:25:37 other, making it easier to test and
19:25:39 maintain. Let's suppose for instance in
19:25:41 your application, you can inject
19:25:44 services like a user service into
19:25:46 controllers without having to manually
19:25:49 manage how they are created. Next one is
19:25:52 Typescript benefits. NestJS fully
19:25:55 embraces Typescript giving you tools
19:25:57 like compile time safety. Now this means

19:26:04 autocomp completion and indepth

19:58:39 you'll learn how to manage complex
19:58:41 dependencies effectively. So now let's
19:58:43 understand how dependency injection
19:58:45 works in NJS. Well, dependency injection
19:58:49 in SJS is the process of passing the
19:58:51 required dependencies which are like
19:58:53 services data into a class. Now, this
19:58:56 will allow you to keep your classes
19:58:58 decoupled from each other. Meaning that
19:59:01 they don't have to worry about how to
19:59:03 create or manage the instances of the
19:59:04 objects that they need. Now when your
19:59:07 application starts up, Nest builds a
19:59:09 container called an IOC container to
19:59:12 manage all your providers like services
19:59:14 and other classes and their dependencies
19:59:17 as well. Now let's understand how
19:59:19 dependency injection works step by step.
19:59:22 So you have seen these decorators like
19:59:24 injectable right now. These decorators
19:59:26 are used to mark a class as something
19:59:29 that can be injected into some other
19:59:31 parts of your app like controllers which
19:59:33 I told you before. Now when a class such
19:59:35 as a controller declares a dependency in
19:59:38 its constructor like a service, Nest
19:59:40 will look for the appropriate provider
19:59:43 usually by class name and it will inject
19:59:45 it. The default scope for providers is
19:59:48 singleton meaning one instance of a
19:59:50 service will be shared across your
19:59:52 application. But you can also set it for
19:59:54 creating new instances for each request
19:59:57 or whenever the service is injected. So
20:00:00 here's an example of how dependency

20:24:44 Basically, guards are responsible for
20:24:46 controlling access to your they act like
20:24:48 bouncers at a club deciding whether a
20:24:50 request should be allowed to proceed or
20:24:52 not based on specific conditions like
20:24:54 authentication, user roles or feature

20:25:01 told you guards are classes that
20:25:02 implement the can activate interface. So
20:25:05 basically guards are classes that
20:25:06 implement the can activate interface.
20:25:09 They are executed before the request
20:25:11 reaches the route handler and are useful
20:25:13 for authentication, authorization and
20:25:16 custom logic checks. So let's see how
20:25:18 guard works. Well, guards check if a
20:25:21 request should proceed based on a custom
20:25:23 logic or not. Now if a guard returns
20:25:25 true, the request is allowed to proceed.
20:25:28 And if the guard returns false or throws
20:25:30 an exception, example unauthorized
20:25:33 exception, the request is then blocked.
20:25:35 Guards can be applied globally for all
20:25:38 the roots at the controller level or an
20:25:40 individual routes using the at the
20:25:42 rateate use guards decorator. So let's
